metadata.career_relevance: Every interview that asks about exceptions goes here: 'What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?' is THE most common Java exception question at every level. The `throws` keyword appears in every JDBC, file I/O, and network method signature you'll use. Understanding why Java's designers made this split — and when each is appropriate — separates engineers who understand Java from those who just copy-paste try-catch. After this topic, you'll read any method signature and instantly know whether you MUST handle its exception or can choose to.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll understand the full Throwable hierarchy, clearly distinguish checked from unchecked exceptions, use the `throws` keyword to declare exceptions, know when to catch vs declare, and be able to answer 'checked vs unchecked' in any interview context.

concept_explanation.throwable_hierarchy:
**The complete Java exception hierarchy:**

```
Throwable
├── Error                          ← System-level failures, DON'T CATCH
│   ├── OutOfMemoryError
│   ├── StackOverflowError
│   ├── VirtualMachineError
│   └── AssertionError
└── Exception
    ├── IOException                ← CHECKED
    │   └── FileNotFoundException
    ├── SQLException               ← CHECKED
    ├── CloneNotSupportedException ← CHECKED
    ├── RuntimeException           ← UNCHECKED (and all its subclasses)
    │   ├── NullPointerException
    │   ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
    │   ├── IllegalArgumentException
    │   ├── IllegalStateException
    │   ├── NumberFormatException
    │   ├── ClassCastException
    │   ├── ArithmeticException
    │   └── UnsupportedOperationException
    └── ...
```

**The rule**: any Exception that is NOT a `RuntimeException` or `Error` is **checked**. `RuntimeException` and all its subclasses are **unchecked**.
concept_explanation.checked_unchecked_philosophy:
**Why Java has both — the design philosophy:**

Java's creators believed:
- External failures (file missing, network down) are EXPECTED and RECOVERABLE → checked
- Programming bugs (null deref, array out of bounds) are UNEXPECTED and indicate broken code → unchecked

The checked exception system tells you: 'This call depends on something outside your program. You MUST have a plan for when it fails.'

**Modern view (post-Java 5 community shift)**: Many frameworks (Spring, Kotlin, modern Java) now prefer unchecked exceptions because:
- Checked exceptions in method signatures spread through the call chain (every caller must declare or catch)
- They make code verbose
- Streams and lambdas can't easily throw checked exceptions

Spring wraps all SQLExceptions in unchecked `DataAccessException`. Spring's HTTP client throws unchecked `RestClientException`.

**The practical takeaway**: Java's core APIs (I/O, JDBC, threads) use checked exceptions — you MUST handle them. Application code tends toward unchecked — IllegalArgumentException, IllegalStateException. Know both; use each in its context.

visual_aid.description: Large tree diagram of the Throwable hierarchy. Root: Throwable. Two main branches: Error (red — don't catch) and Exception (green — two sub-branches). Exception left branch: RuntimeException with all unchecked subclasses listed (NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, etc.) — labeled UNCHECKED. Exception right branch: IOException, SQLException, etc. — labeled CHECKED. Decision flowchart below: 'Does it extend RuntimeException?' YES → Unchecked (compiler optional). NO + extends Exception → Checked (compiler mandatory). Right column: 'throws vs catch decision' flowchart — 'Can I recover?' YES → catch. 'Does caller have more context?' YES → throws.
visual_aid.alt_text: Throwable hierarchy tree distinguishing checked, unchecked, and Errors with decision flowcharts

worked_example.scenario: Build a multi-layer application that correctly propagates checked exceptions through layers — a realistic pattern from every Spring/JDBC application. A data access layer reads from a 'file database', a service layer processes it, and a controller layer handles failures for the user.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Three-layer architecture:
1. `DataAccess.loadUsers(String filename)` — throws IOException (file operation)
2. `UserService.getActiveUsers(String filename)` — catches IOException, throws custom ServiceException
3. `UserController.listUsers()` — catches ServiceException, returns user-friendly message

Demonstrates the real exception propagation pattern in layered applications.

step_by_step_solution.action: Data access layer — declares checked IOException
Service layer — catches IOException, throws domain exception
Demonstrate with good and bad file paths

worked_example.expected_output:
[ERROR] Could not load user data from: nonexistent.txt
Service temporarily unavailable.
Found 2 active users: [Aisha ACTIVE, Priya ACTIVE]
things_to_notice: DataAccess.loadUsers() declares `throws IOException` — it's a checked exception. The compiler would reject this method if it didn't declare or handle IOException.

UserService.getActiveUsers() catches the IOException and rethrows as ServiceException (unchecked). This is the standard Spring pattern — the service layer translates infrastructure exceptions into domain exceptions.
UserController.listUsers() catches ServiceException — no throws needed (unchecked). The controller is the boundary between the application and the user/caller.
The cause chain is preserved: ServiceException wraps IOException wraps FileNotFoundException. `e.getCause()` retrieves the original IOException.
The error is printed to System.err (not System.out) — standard for error logging. User sees only the friendly message on stdout.
This three-layer exception handling pattern is foundational to Spring MVC, Spring Boot, and virtually every professional Java application.

industry_examples.company_context: Spring Boot REST APIs (every Java backend)
industry_examples.use_case: Spring wraps JDBC's checked SQLExceptions in unchecked DataAccessException. Your repository methods (findById, save) are unchecked — Spring's designers chose this deliberately. But the underlying JDBC driver throws checked SQLException. Spring handles the translation. Understanding why Spring made this choice requires understanding checked vs unchecked.

industry_examples.company_context: File processing pipelines (Zerodha trade data, HDFC statements)
industry_examples.use_case: Importing bank statements or trade histories involves reading files (IOException), parsing dates (ParseException), and database writes (SQLException) — all checked. These methods declare `throws IOException, ParseException, SQLException`. The calling service catches all three and produces a meaningful import result: 'Imported 1234 records. 5 malformed rows skipped.'

Android's APIs use checked exceptions for anything that can fail externally: BluetoothException, IOException for file access, InterruptedException for background threads. Google's Android guidelines are explicit: catch checked exceptions at the appropriate layer, convert to unchecked for propagation through UI layers.

applications.interview_relevance:
**THE most common exception interview question** — at every level:

- **'What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?'**
  → Checked: compiler forces handling; extend Exception but not RuntimeException. Unchecked: optional; extend RuntimeException. Checked = external failures. Unchecked = programming bugs.

- **'Give examples of each.'**
  → Checked: IOException, SQLException. Unchecked: NullPointerException, IllegalArgumentException.

- **'Why does Spring use unchecked exceptions?'**
  → Reduces boilerplate; easier with lambdas/streams; service layer shouldn't be polluted with infrastructure exception types.

- **'What does `throws` do in a method signature?'**
  → Declares that the method can throw a checked exception. Callers must handle or re-declare.

- **'Should you catch RuntimeException?'**
  → Generally no — RuntimeExceptions usually indicate bugs. Fix the bug. Catch specific unchecked exceptions only when you can meaningfully recover.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily checked/unchecked decisions:

- Any file I/O → IOException (checked) — wrap in try-catch or declare throws
- Any JDBC/database operation → SQLException (checked) — handled by framework or caught explicitly
- Parsing dates → ParseException (checked) — catch or declare
- Validating method arguments → IllegalArgumentException (unchecked) — throw when input is bad
- Method precondition violated → IllegalStateException (unchecked)
- Null input where not allowed → NullPointerException (unchecked) or explicitly throw IllegalArgumentException
- Any Spring service method → unchecked by convention (Spring wraps checked for you)

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Thinking all exceptions need try-catch, or that they NEVER need try-catch.
common_gaps.remediation: Simple test: does the exception extend RuntimeException? If yes, unchecked (optional catch). If it extends Exception directly (not via RuntimeException), it's checked — MUST catch or declare throws.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Unnecessary try-catch around RuntimeException; missing try-catch around IOException

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Method calls something that throws IOException but doesn't catch it AND doesn't declare throws — compile error.
common_gaps.remediation: Two choices when your method calls checked-exception-throwing code: (1) wrap in try-catch, or (2) add `throws CheckedException` to your method signature.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Catching StackOverflowError or OutOfMemoryError and trying to recover.
common_gaps.remediation: Errors indicate the JVM is in a broken state. You cannot meaningfully recover. Let them propagate. Fix the root cause (infinite recursion, memory leak).

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Confusing `throws` (declaration) with `throw` (statement). `throws` in method signature = declares it might happen. `throw new X()` = actually throws it.
common_gaps.remediation: `throws` is a METHOD SIGNATURE keyword: `public void foo() throws IOException`. `throw` is a STATEMENT inside a method body: `throw new IOException('...')`.

key_takeaways: Throwable → Error (don't catch) + Exception → checked + RuntimeException (unchecked)
Checked exceptions: extend Exception (not RuntimeException). Compiler forces handling.
Unchecked exceptions: extend RuntimeException. Compiler is silent.
Errors: system failures (OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError). NEVER catch.
`throws` on method signature: declares the method can throw a checked exception. Caller must handle or re-declare.
Catch when you can recover. Declare throws when the caller should decide.
Common checked: IOException, SQLException, ParseException, InterruptedException.
Spring converts checked SQLExceptions to unchecked DataAccessException — deliberate design choice.
The 'checked = external failure, unchecked = programming bug' mental model is the key insight.

self_check_questions.question: Without looking it up — is NumberFormatException checked or unchecked? How do you know?
self_check_questions.answer: Unchecked. It's a `RuntimeException` subclass. You can verify: when you write `Integer.parseInt('abc')` in code without a try-catch, it compiles fine. The compiler doesn't complain = unchecked. Also: NumberFormatException represents a PROGRAMMING issue (you passed a bad string) rather than an external failure.

self_check_questions.question: A method calls `new FileReader('data.txt')` but doesn't have a try-catch. What are the two legal ways to fix the compile error?
Why did Spring's designers choose to make DataAccessException (database errors) unchecked?
self_check_questions.answer: SQLException (JDBC's checked exception) forced every service method to declare `throws SQLException` or wrap in try-catch. This meant database concerns leaked through every layer. With an unchecked DataAccessException, service methods don't need to declare it. Code is cleaner. Callers can handle database errors at the appropriate level (controller, global error handler) without every intermediate method being aware of it. Also enables clean use with lambdas and streams.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: **Custom Exceptions** — designing your own exception types for your application's domain. Instead of throwing `RuntimeException('Insufficient funds')`, you throw `InsufficientFundsException(accountId, attempted, available)`. Domain-specific exceptions make code self-documenting, enable precise catching, and are the professional way to signal errors from your APIs.
